DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-28-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Eric Young's demurrer to Plaintiff Paul Henry's third amended complaint ("TAC") is overruled.

Young demurs to Plaintiff's causes of action for breach of contract, fraud, wrongful termination, and violations of the Labor Code on the basis that he was not a party to the contract and did not employ Plaintiff. However, Plaintiff has cured the defects in his second amended complaint by alleging facts regarding Young's liability under the alter ego doctrine.

Plaintiff now alleges: there is a unity of interest between the entity defendants and Young; Young intended to use the corporate entities as a shield against personal liability; Young dominated the entity defendants; Young and the entity defendants used the same office and attorney; the entities were inadequately capitalized; Young used the entity defendants to procure labor, services and goods for another person or entity; Young commingled funds; Young treated the entity defendants' assets as his own; defendants failed to maintain adequate corporate records; the entity defendants are a mere shell and conduit; there were "misrepresentations of the identity of the responsible ownership, management and financial interest, and concealment of personal business activities"; there was "a disregard of legal formalities and the failure to maintain arm's length relationships among related entities"; and recognizing the separate existence of the entities would promote injustice. (TAC, 5.) Assuming these facts can be proved, Young may be held liable under the alter ego doctrine. (See Rutherford Holdings, LLC v. Plaza Del Rey, (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 221, 235-36; First Western Bank & Trust Co. v. Bookasta (1968) 267 Cal.App.2d 910, 915-916.)

Young also demurs to Plaintiff's fraud cause of action on the basis that Plaintiff's allegations lack the necessary specificity. But Plaintiff's allegations satisfy the heightened pleading requirements for fraud: he alleges "the names of the persons who made the allegedly fraudulent representations, their authority to speak, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when it was said or written." (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 645; TAC, 23-24.)

Based on the foregoing, Young's demurrer is overruled in its entirety.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com mailto:maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-16-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS ERIC YOUNG and CEREGO, INC.'s Demurrer to Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Cerego, Inc. and Eric Young's demurrers are overruled and sustained as follows:

Defendant Cerego's demurrer is overruled as to Plaintiff's second cause of action. Plaintiff's allegations satisfy the heightened pleading requirements for fraud: he alleges "the names of the persons who made the allegedly fraudulent representations, their authority to speak, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when it was said or written." (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 645; SAC, 14-15.)

Cerego's demurrer is overruled as to Plaintiff's third cause of action. Plaintiff properly pleads wrongful termination. (SAC, 22-26.)

Defendant Young's demurrer is sustained with leave to amend as to all of Plaintiff's causes of action. Plaintiff is granted leave to amend to allege facts regarding Young's liability under the alter ego doctrine. Currently, the complaint states only that "Eric Young is the alter ego of the Cerego defendant entities." (SAC, 8.) This allegation is conclusory. (See Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 74 [finding that alter ego allegation "fail[ed] to include facts showing a unity of interest and a resultant injustice, prerequisites to an alter ego theory."]; Rutherford Holdings, LLC v. Plaza Del Rey, (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 221, 235-36.)

Order signed.

Defendants Cerego, Llc And Eric Young'S Motion For Leave To Withdraw Answers And Affirmative Defenses To Unverified First Amended Complaint Of Paul Henry (Per Ex Parte Order Filed 8/4/2021)
Off Calendar Aug-16-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. BENJAMIN MARTIN, Esq. of Ari Law, P.C. (Tel: 415-830-9968) for plaintiff;
Atty. KENNETH R. VAN VLECK, Esq. of GCA Law Partners LLP (Tel: 650-428-3900) for defendants Cerego, Inc. and Eric Young.

Hearing Re: Defendants Cerego, LLC and Eric Young's Motion For Leave To Withdraw Answers And Affirmative Defenses To Unverified First Amended Complaint Of Paul Henry.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Cerego, LLC and Eric Young's motion for leave to withdraw answers and affirmative defenses to unverified first amended complaint of Paul Henry is granted. Defendants seek to withdraw their answers, apparently in order to allow plaintiff to take their defaults. Although this course of action is admittedly unusual, Plaintiff does not identify any authority that would prevent Defendants from doing so.

Plaintiff "conditionally" opposes the motion on the ground that he should be granted leave to file and serve a second amended complaint before the Court rules on Defendants' motion in order to cure a defect in his current first amended complaint: its failure to demand a specific dollar amount. Plaintiff has filed a motion for leave to amend that is currently noticed for hearing on September 17, 2021, and also requests the Court to advance the hearing date to August 16. Plaintiffs' requests are denied. If defendants withdraw their answers, nothing will force plaintiff to take their defaults or seek entry of a void default judgment. Nor is the relief plaintiff seeks necessary to avoid prejudice to plaintiff, since defendants have appeared and may be served through their counsel of record; thus, their current whereabouts and amenability to service is irrelevant.

Order signed.
